sections.content: The Vulgate is St. Jerome's Latin translation of the Bible, completed around 405 AD. It was the Bible of the Western Church for over a thousand years and remains the official Latin text. Reading familiar Gospel passages in Latin builds confidence and connects you to the scriptural tradition of the Church.

sections.title: The Prologue of St. John (John 1:1-5)
sections.content:
This passage is read as the Last Gospel at the end of every Traditional Latin Mass.

In princípio erat Verbum, et Verbum erat apud Deum, et Deus erat Verbum.
In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God.

Hoc erat in princípio apud Deum.
The same was in the beginning with God.

Ómnia per ipsum facta sunt: et sine ipso factum est nihil, quod factum est.
All things were made by Him: and without Him was made nothing that was made.

In ipso vita erat, et vita erat lux hóminum:
In Him was life, and the life was the light of men:

et lux in ténebris lucet, et ténebræ eam non comprehendérunt.
and the light shineth in darkness, and the darkness did not comprehend it.

sections.title: The Annunciation (Luke 1:28-33)
sections.content:
The origin of the Ave Maria and the moment of the Incarnation.

Et ingréssus ángelus ad eam dixit: Ave, grátia plena: Dóminus tecum: benedícta tu in muliéribus.
And the angel being come in, said unto her: Hail, full of grace, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women.

Quæ cum audísset, turbáta est in sermóne eius, et cogitábat qualis esset ista salutátio.
Who having heard, was troubled at his saying, and thought within herself what manner of salutation this should be.

Et ait ángelus ei: Ne tímeas, María, invenísti enim grátiam apud Deum.
And the angel said to her: Fear not, Mary, for thou hast found grace with God.

Ecce concípies in útero, et páries fílium, et vocábis nomen eius Iesum.
Behold thou shalt conceive in thy womb, and shalt bring forth a son, and thou shalt call His name Jesus.

sections.title: The Magnificat (Luke 1:46-55)
sections.content:
Mary's canticle, sung daily at Vespers. You already know this from the Divine Office.

Magníficat ánima mea Dóminum:
My soul doth magnify the Lord:

Et exsultávit spíritus meus in Deo salutári meo.
And my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Savior.

Quia respéxit humilitátem ancíllæ suæ: ecce enim ex hoc beátam me dicent omnes generatiónes.
Because He hath regarded the humility of His handmaid: for behold from henceforth all generations shall call me blessed.

Quia fecit mihi magna qui potens est: et sanctum nomen eius.
Because He that is mighty hath done great things to me: and holy is His name.

Et misericórdia eius a progénie in progénies timéntibus eum.
And His mercy is from generation unto generations, to them that fear Him.

The Vulgate uses the Latin of the late Roman Empire — simpler than Cicero's Classical Latin. St. Jerome deliberately chose clarity over literary elegance because he was translating Sacred Scripture for the whole Church, not composing literature for elites. This makes it surprisingly accessible.

You have now read three of the most important passages in the Latin Bible: the theological prologue of John's Gospel (read at every Mass), the Annunciation (the origin of the Hail Mary), and the Magnificat (sung daily at Vespers). These texts will meet you again and again in the liturgy. Each time, you will understand a little more.
